insights: 92.4% of assets (462 out of 500) have warranty expiration dates falling before the end of a standard 3-year employment tenure, indicating systemic warranty coverage misalignment across the organization.

The average warranty coverage gap is 475.6 days per asset, meaning warranties expire over 15 months earlier than typical employee tenure — a material risk to operational continuity and unplanned maintenance cost exposure.

Asset procurement is overwhelmingly pre-hire: 62.6% of assets were purchased before employee start dates, with the latest post-hire purchase occurring only 6 days after hire — confirming a rigid, tenure-agnostic procurement cadence.

Warranty inefficiency is uniformly distributed across all departments (85–100% inefficient rates) and all model categories (87–100% inefficient rates), ruling out departmental or hardware-specific drivers and pointing to a centralized policy-level root cause.

No meaningful variation in warranty coverage efficiency exists by department or model category, eliminating opportunities for targeted optimization and confirming that remediation must occur at the procurement governance or warranty term negotiation level.

The near-total absence of post-hire procurement (only 37.4% purchased after start date, none beyond +6 days) indicates procurement timing is decoupled from actual staffing timelines, undermining warranty value realization.
summary: The analysis definitively reveals a systemic warranty coverage failure: 92.4% of assets lose warranty protection well before employees complete a standard 3-year tenure, with an average gap of 476 days — exposing the organization to significant unplanned repair costs and service disruption risk. This inefficiency is not driven by departmental practices or asset type differences, but by a rigid, pre-hire procurement pattern where 62.6% of assets are purchased before employees start, and virtually none are procured more than 6 days after hire. The uniformity of the problem across all organizational units confirms it stems from enterprise-wide procurement policies or vendor contract terms, not local execution. To optimize asset lifecycle value, the immediate focus must shift from diagnostic analysis to revising procurement timing protocols and renegotiating minimum warranty durations aligned with expected employment tenure.
